Wow before I get to my race review I've just gotta say good job to everyone today... so many people stepping up, racing just like we planned... oh man... shoutouts: Karen Condon, Martha, Kelly Kuo, Katie, Fran, Becky Idell... they all know why they get them and everyone else awesome job too! Hard not to feel good when you WIN! Getting into specifics: running uphill hurt . I am really bad at it. This week though I was able to utilize the downhills more like I am good at. Followed the plan pretty perfectly--hard off the line, relaxed through mile one, 7 Wellesley in front of me then. Started picking off blue uniforms in the woods (mile 2), came out of the woods a few places up including two Wellesley, doing the hill a second time was quite a pain but I didn't lose any places that I can remember. Took off after the U-turn, coming back downhill I think I passed three or four girls in the final 600...including bearing down on the Wellesley girl in front of me, almost caught Wheaton too but she was too far up. Really gave everything in the final kick; I could feel it in my stomach. Also, went back to the original lucky hair tie. Gotta stick w/that. Quick goals breakdown: top 14, no (18th); beat Wellesley's #3; no (KC got her though); beat Sarah Bard, yes; use the downhills and my kick, yes. Not on the goal list but definitely a plus...PR! Don't like to overanalyze the last meet of the season but...I'll comment briefly: Not a bad race...time was poor but doesn't reflect much because of the conditions. (Windy, ~40 degrees, frozen ground, tough course) I think 82nd place is a pretty solid showing. I actually think I ran pretty hard because I was pretty wiped afterwords. I felt like I was racing pretty well while I was running. Pretty much in control, was able to make moves and accelerate when I needed to (aka: wasn't so dead that I was slowing down the whole time), hung on to Karen Kinnaman who was racing right in front of me/with me until the last 600 or so. The hills didn't brutalize me as much as they did last time on the course, or even last meet. I think from where I was at the second mile I picked up about 10 places, including somehow digging out some semblance of a kick in the last 75m or so, I think I got three people there. All in all, I could say that I would like to have a better 6k time, but I don't think I could have run it today. Cool parts: 1. staying in a hotel (hooray luggage carts and elevators), 2. using CHIP TIMING! Dude, I think I got placed ahead of this girl only because my foot came in before hers...boo yah! 3. Picnics! Yay! 4. Fan club driving out to watch us 5. Men's coach Halston Taylor talking to me after the race and saying he thought I did well and had a good season. 6. This season... Splits: crazy

oh man! first race in a while that felt good... which means this recap is going to be detailed ;-) It was a screwy-length track (180 yds, I believe) so I decided to take advantage of that and screw the splits and run for place; I figure I'm getting too obsessed with my times anyway. I was on the outside (of the 4 lane track), but got off to a good start, cleared the crowd and settled into 5th by lap 2, behind two Williams girls and two RPI girls. Somewhere in the first five laps one of the RPI girls dropped, one of the Williams girls moved up, and I tucked in right behind the other two, who sort of traded off the lead, sometimes going shoulder to shoulder. I was trying to count my laps; there were 16 2/3 and I had planned to try to pick it up when I had 8 to go. At that point though, I was dropping back from the two a few meters and decided that I would just try to stick with them. One or two laps later I lost count and couldn't understand the official so I just stayed with the tucking-in plan. HBH kept telling me to draft, which sounded all right with me, so I just hung out there. At some point HBH told me that if I was going to go, I had to do it then, and since I didn't really know how many laps I had left (it was 3 or 4), I figured, what the heck. So I started moving, and even though it was the beginning of a turn, I got around both girls pretty easily and didn't feel either of them come with me. In hindsight, I must have had 2.5 laps left, because when I went across the line the next time ahead of the two girls, I wasn't sure if I had one or two laps left. I thought the guy may have said 1 to me, so I started to go even harder (athough we had already picked it up), then I was thinking maybe I had another after this, so I stopped accelerating (but didn't slow down) and really just slammed it when I finally did have one to go. So basically, it was good. I responded to conditions instead of trying to control the whole thing, and I kicked earlier than I probably would have otherwise, but just like at the UMass Dartmouth race in XC, it worked out for the better, because I held off both girls for 2nd place and a PR.

oh man, I did it. The goal was 19:29 for the qualifying mark and the rookie record, but I was SO scared I wouldn't be able to do it. I knew I was capable physically, but I just didn't know if it would happen. But it did, and man, I'm so relieved. Ok, so, the recap (once again, good race=long summary)... So I woke up this morning and was suddenly pumped, after a week of feeling pretty down. Deanna called last night, which was exciting, and I dunno I just suddenly felt good. Mentally, I decided to treat the race like an XC race; 6:10, 12:30 were the splits I wanted to hit. The only other thing I wanted to think about was not to forget that indoor tracks are smaller, and that even when someone is a straightaway up on you, it's really not that far. I came through 8 laps in 6:03, I believe, and then 12:20. A bit fast but it wasn't hurting so I kept at it. There was a little pack right behind me; I didn't know how many people but from the cheering I heard Jen, Nancy, and someone from another school. In the last mile the mystery third girl kept clipping me in the heel, not on purpose, obviously, but I figured that she was just sitting on my ass and wasn't going to pass me. It was annoying, but I can't blame her because I would have done the same had I been able. But I couldn't slow down to force her to pass, since I was going for a time. With 800 to go (counted my laps in 8's this time so I wouldn't mess up) the girl passed me and I was starting to feel pretty beat. I was slowing, hoping that I had given myself enough cushion to still qualify, when Jen came up on me with 2.5 laps to go. Man, am I glad she was there. Suddenly I remembered that I was racing, and picked it up a bit, and when I crossed the line I realized "dude! 2 laps is only 400 m, and in high school I always had something left for the last lap..." and just gunned it. With 150 to go I saw the Brandeis girl ahead of me (the clipper) and remembered my "only other" thought, and just started chasing. As I hit the turn I was accelerating pretty hard and I sort of knew I had her, then coming off the turn I went by and finished in front, 6th place. So stoked... And what made it even better were all of the other stellar races of the day... · Jen "my goal was to break 20 but, oh look! I broke 19:30 and qualified for NE D3's instead" Gaugler · Martha "well I'm always a stud and plus it's my birthday" Buckley (with the win and the NCAA provisional qualfier) · Nancy "sub 20, mad PR, even though I almost didn't race" Benedetti · Betsy "ha! I got you" Eames

I don't know what to make of it. I feel like it was good, but not good enough. I don't even feel like it took a lot out of me. On the one hand, you can't complain about a PR, right? But on the other... it would be a lie to say I am not disappointed. Shooting for sub-11, and instead I'm two seconds away from a rookie record and 6 seconds from the qualifying time. The two seconds I could have had anywhere . Such as...the chunks in the middle where I slowed to 92 for the quarter, or the last 3 or so laps, where I just didn't feel like kicking or racing the girl that beat me, or maybe I could have had two seconds if I wasn't getting tailed and sucked off of again... I know that is something I have to learn to deal with and complaining is no good but... eh. I guess the rookie record isn't important; the question is could I have gotten the 11:03 to qualify for NE D3's? I think physically I was definitely capable. But there was something missing in my head, some unwillingness to push any harder. I don't know. I am just not risky enough in my races. That's why I do better in the longer events, I think, because I don't necessarily have to risk anything, I just run hard and hang on for as long as possible. But I need to learn to actually go faster . Well, at least now I'm sure I could hit the sub-12 3200 that I was after all last year. Interestingly enough, I am not disappointed. I did horribly, I ran slowly, but I could not have run any faster that day. There are times in some races, when you are giving up mentally, but some shout or move on another racer's part inspires you to suck it up and keep going, but every time one of those moments happened today, there was nothing in my body to call upon. I had no resources with which to run a race. I don't know why that was; I could pick apart my training or any number of things that were different about this season and maybe I would find an answer, but the fact is that in retrospect, I don't see any way that I could have run a sub-19 on Saturday, Feb. 21, 2004. I do believe it is within my physical capabilities to run that fast.

Well there was no wimping out today, which has been a trend through some of my races. It was crazy windy down the whole back 200 all day; times were slow across the board. Even though I didn't get the ECAC mark today, I'm pretty sure that my race was worth around 11:55 in good conditions, so hopefully next week I'll just lay it out. I'm still looking down at the barriers too much, but I think my hurdling is getting better. Ran a solid race for 5th place, matching my seed, which was fine since there really wasn't anyone within 20 seconds of me either way. The 1500 was 50 minutes later, which was a bit harsh. I was seeded 8th in the fast heat, but Julia had a NT and was in the slow heat, so I was trying to bust out 6th or 7th. With the wind I really was only running for place, plus it was a team scoring meet, so I tried to tuck in with the pack and just maintain. Worked out for the first lap, came through in 80, but then I really started feeling it. Everything hurt, two or three girls went around me, and I tried my best to hang with them. I knew I wasn't in last, by 2 laps to go I wasn't sure what I had left. I'm really happy though, because at that point I usually just start "coasting," and sort of resign myself to whatever is going to happen. But this time I actually kept my heart in it and still tried for places. On the final 300 there was a Smith girl and a Coast Guard girl about 75m up; HBH was yelling "they're coming back to you," and when I looked up, it definitely looked like the Smith girl was. The USCGA girl started pulling away and I just sort of mentally started following her; by 100 to go I was just barely within kicking reach of the Smith girl. At probably the last possible second, I found one more gear. Smith was teetering out into lane 2 and I figured that one a straightaway it wasn't rude to pass on the inside, so I just stared at the finish line and shot up lane 1. 5:22.38 to 5:22.71, good for 7th in the heat. Unfortunately, Julia's heat actually had a second person who should have been seeded higher, and I wound up 9th, just one place out of scoring. But I can't control that.

Damn, that was frustrating. My fever came back last night, and even though it was gone completely by the time I went to bed, and never came back today, I was still left with nothing. My muscles were quivering a little in the morning, but I didn't want to psyche myself out (I was already losing confidence) so I just played it off. I figured it was my last race if I didn't run, and possibly not my last race if I did, so why not? Well... it ended up being my last race. I don't know, there's not much use discussing the race itself because it was not meant to be a legitimate shot. I went out fine, even a few seconds under the plan for the mile (88/2:57/6:10 or thereabouts). Right after the mile though, right after I came through the finish and saw the 3 laps to go sign, I just turned to jello. I wanted to drop the race then and there, but I figured I'd just go around to the 200 and ask HBH if I could. Of course I didn't ask, so I just kept running, but every time I came up to a barrier I literally thought I was going to cry. I ended up nicking my knee on one of the barriers actually, that's how lazy I got, and apparently I was swerving on the straights, which I don't remember at all... yeah basically I just slogged it in with little energy or heart. I finished the race and really did break down and start crying; I think that's the first time in my life that's ever happened. Really sucks that I never got a really good shot at this event. 11:55 was totally within reach, and it sucks that all this work and all the fitness that I gained is going to shit, and that I have no more chances until next year. Definitely NOT ready for this season to be over after 5 races.

Hoooooooly sh*t! I don't know if there's any way to describe this race! It was so insanely hardcore, and SO MUCH FUN! Seriously, it was like, steeplchase times a hundred. In fact, there was a bit of steeplechasing involved, but it was more like steeple the way the horses do it... So this thing is basically a 10k obstacle course on the Camp Pendleton Marine Base, just north of here. There are like half a dozen mud pits, water crossings, two 5ft walls (covered in mud) to climb over, a tunnel to crawl through, basically craziness all over the place. I've been trying to do this thing for years, and finally this year, Amy, Tina, Julie and Brigitte were all doing it so I signed up too. And it was so awesome! Ok, let me try to describe the course: The whole thing is one big dirt loop, and all of the obstacles are added by the race organizers sort of "on top". So we start off, and about 400m in, they started spraying fire hoses at us. I don't really know why, that didn't add to the race much, but whatever. Then we get to the dirt trail, and we start in, and within a minute there is a pit of loose sand that we had to crawl through on our hands and knees. Also a bit unnecessary, in my opinion, but everything got better from there. After that, there was a little one of those tire obstacle things, and then the first mud pit. It was about knee deep, so you could pick up your legs and still sort of run through it. Then there was just more running for a while; there was about a 3 mile long hill in the middle of the course, but only part of it was particularly steep. At the top of the hill there was a set of hay bales, and then just a lot more running as we wound down the hill. A couple more mud pits were thrown in there somewhere along flat stretches too. About four miles in things started to get crazy. There weren't any mile markers so I didn't know that I was that far, but whatever. So after the hill I come up to the first wall. It's basically just that: a wooden wall across the course, five feet tall, with a pool of mud leading up to and away from it. My first attempt at vaulting myself over proved unsuccessful when my feet got sucked into the mud, but I managed to not sink in and get myself over on the second try. After the wall, I ran a little ways on a trail, then as I turned a corner I started to hear, "It's three feet deep, no diving!" And when I can finally see, I realize that we have to cross what is essentially a small corner of a lake. No joke. It must have been like 40 yards of waist deep water that I had to wade through. I acutally tried swimming it for a little bit, when my quads got insanely tired (as if the previous mud and hills had left them fresh). On the other side, we had to come out this pretty steep, muddy bank. Right about there was when I first started to feel how incredibly dead my legs were. Like, I said, there were no mile markers, but I heard someone mention that we had about 2 miles left. I knew there were at least two more obstacles, and I figured I could just stick it out (I had been running hard, but obviously not racing, the whole time). There was another small hill at this point, which was sort of disheartning, because I didn't remember it from the course map. Then a second climbing wall... again a surprise to me. But after that, Marines were telling us that there was only one mud pit left, and about 1.5 miles, so I kept grinding. There were the tunnels to crawl through, in which I managed to pass this one woman that had been in front of me for a while--I had a vague goal to win my age group, but she looked older anyway so passing her was just sort of unintentional. The tunnels were on top of a hill, and going down there started to be more Marines cheering and saying it was all downhill. Well it wasn't. One last surprise feature was a short, steep hill that also seemed to escape the map. Of course, a hill by itself is so civilian, so to spice life up a bit, they put a couple of hoses at the top and made the whole thing a big, muddy river that we had to scramble up. That actually was the last hill, and coming down it there started to be a couple of spectators scattered about, so I figured I must be near the finish. I picked it up a bit at that thought, and a little more when some people started yelling to me that I was the third woman. I finally reached the last mudpit, about 30 ft long, with flags over the top so that you had to crawl through it on your hands and knees. And that was it. From there it was like a 50 ft dash (or stumble) to the finish. I came across the line exhausted, in 56th place overall, 3rd woman and 1st in the 15-19 women's group. It was only when I stopped that I realized that my knees were bleeding, and when I finally got my shoes off (I needed a Marine to cut off the duck tape), I realized that my feet were bleeding too. First time my feet have ever bled in a race. There were also 7-10 minutes of delerium in there, that I don't exactly remember, but by the time I was coherent again, all of my friends had finished and their parents were swarming over to take pictures. What a great day... Results (Ctrl-F to find me) Course Map Article from Runner's World

what an interesting run. Decided to run to Charlestown and follow the Freedom trial to Boston Common. When I got to Bunker Hill, the museum was open and then I saw a sign saying that you could take stairs to the top of the monument. So I did...all 265 of them. Heh... not a bad view but it was pretty gross-feeling to be at the top of a brick building that's about 20 feet square inside. After that I took the Trail to it's other end and then back on the Esplanade, taking another running break to play in Frog Pond at the Commons. It was 10 o'clock before I headed out this morning so it was damned hot while I was running, so by the end I was dogging it a bit. I decided that to make up for it, I'd go to the track and lay down a hard 400. Plus it's just that point in the summer where I get curious, so after some stretching and a minute of rest, I went out and droped a 79. Not bad
extras: none, i was pretty beat (and lazy). I've been stretching more thoguh the past few days and my foot's been hurting less, hey...

comments: ran out to Summit hill to see what it was all about. found out that it was a fatty hill, too steep for any worthwhile repeats. Ran up the sidestreet next to it 9xs, moderate, hard, hardest and then did Summit once. HAPPY BIRTHDAY, DAD

WOOHOO! holy crap! Finally got our first real downpour... took off around 8:30 am on 3 hours of sleep (last night was the WTP girls' last night here) and just went nuts. Was talking to myself, got soaked, found every puddle, got soaked... On another note I think I'm going to try to start doing my normal runs a bit faster if I can. I hit 50 miles this week (yEah) and didn't feel too sluggish, so the goal is to have everything at 8 min pace by the end of the summer.

whoa, yeah, one thing that isn't a good idea: waking up at 11, deciding to eat ice cream for breakfast, and then going for an 8 mile run. The churning in my stomach went away after 3 miles or so, and the rest of the run ended up being ok. solid pace, I think it was more than 8 miles
4x5' tempo, around 7' pace for the tempo parts :( I dunno... i was tired... still gotta catch up on the rest

20 min swim, 20 min aquajog

biked to Jamaica Pond w/Jen, then we ran to Franklin Park. Did the course, jogged around a bit, wandered around the Arboretum, and back. Sort of a surreal feeling run. On the one hand, it didn't feel like I was doing a long run, even while I was doing it. Like, at the end, I didn't feel like I had just run almost two hours. We didn't stop that much; once to stretch and once for water, and the pace around the course was actually down near 8/8:15, so I don't know why it felt like such a junky run. Can't decide if it was a good run or not. Also feeling a little bit like that in the general sense; even though I am into "high mileage" I don't feel all cut and in shape like I did two weeks ago. I guess I've had more dessert than normal this week, and not sleeping too regularly since the girls are gone. Hopefully when I go home I can focus and feel fit again.

BU bridge loop then over to the commons. Lots of police and media around... apparently someone named "Kerry" lives across the street, ha. 4 moderate, 3 hard, 3 harder, walked down the hill after the first two sets. felt pretty strong. normally would have doubled but I have to pack and stuff today
home! felt surprisingly fresh, despite all the travelling. leaving for hawaii tomorrow, going to take a real easy week, maybe 5-6 mi/day
Flew into HI around noon island time, explored Summer's neighborhood (Palolo Valley) in the afternoon. pretty hilly around here

Hm, a lot of my running is going to end up being through town on the sidewalks, since i don't have a car, but oh well. Ran down towards the beach and back uphill to the house, about 30 min after dinner though :-(
Guess I haven't really been taking it too easy this week. Legs were a bit tired from walking around so much, but Hawaii is awesome! We snorkeled with sea turtles!

AHH... my lack of ride and my friends' lack of early morning motivation left beginning my run at promptly 12:30 pm, which, in the middle of the Pacific Ocean, means that it's just 85 deg. Only 30 min of the run we actually true hell though, and I got to run around the Diamond Head Crater and through the parks of Waikiki. All in all, it was not spectacular, but I got business done

woke up early to run before my flight home. got a bit of shade as I explored the Palolo valley one more time. Seemed to be always running uphill. my quads are burning now (nighttime) but now I'm back and can properly take care of myself. running on the roads all week didn't feel the greatest, but I'm ok

Went to the bike path and ran w/Dad for the first two miles, which, as expected were fast... Dad took the first one out in 7:50. Turned around and started my tempo, then turned around and doubled back to the first bridge from the beginning. The first two miles tempo were ~6:55, last one 6:40. Would have liked the first two to be down at 6:40 also, but I guess I started off fast. Hope I can shake this perpetually flat feeling when I start racing.

Felt totally fresh, which was really out of the blue. Remembered it was Tuesday so I drove down to the beach to run with the team. Went to Via de la Valle and back with them, then south to Guy Fleming to include a significant hill, then back north to Powerhouse park. Didn't really start slowing down or dogging it anywhere except up the park road. Yay

nothing like seeing a rattlesnake on your run to make you run a few minutes of tempo, heh. did the San Dieguito Lagoon run, plus some in the park. felt nice and snappy again. maybe it's the home cooking.

went to practice again. Dani needed to run hills also so we decided that Broken Hills was an appropriate substitute for repeats. Did drill with the team and my legs were feeling shot because I skipped breakfast, so when we got to the base of the trail I let Dani and Kim lead. Ended up feeling strong the whole way up though; if it wasn't a single file run I could have definitely been up on Dani's shoulder. Just as well that she was in front though, she took us up in 11:44, the fastest I've ever done it, I think. Added on to complete the mileage then did lots of strides at the track on the way home.
extras: spb, ice bath, drills, strides, dsbp

comments: Brenna decided not to do the America's Finest City half marathon, so I took her number and ran it, just so I could have a more exciting long run this week. It was nice; I never like the road races because they're all on the road (imagine that) but the views were cool and it kept me from being bored. Managed to hold back that road racing urge. 7:45 pace average but there was a fatty downhill from mile 2-5 which dropped the pace. Splits: Mile 1-7 // 54:42 Last 10k // 46:47 Results (search for Brenna Williams)

Went across to the other, stealth side of TP state park, and found a trail to do repeats on with Dani. We did two sets of five, walking down between sets. It was a single file trail, so we took turns leading. Managed to press the workout the whole time. On the last one, I left two seconds before her and she chased me down. Nearly got me, but I held off. One of those feel-your-heartbeat-in-your-head finishes. Awesome workout, maybe the best all summer.
I feel awesome! I'm through "hump day" of my big week for the summer and I've felt nothing but fresh and solid throughout. Stoked
easy run on the beach. south to Flat Rock where I took a minute to look at crabs and poke sea anemone in the tide pools. Ran the last 5 min barefoot so I could splash in the water.

Went out to PQ canyon. excluding a bit of a wu/cd, went out in 24:24, back in 20:55. Wanted to come back in 20 but 2/3 of the way back my legs were lagging. it's weird, I feel super strong this summer, but I haven't had my usual control over my tempo runs. Out and backs have always been my favorite just because I can usually blast them so well, but this summer they've been a struggle. Oh well, I ran until my stomach churned, that always means I'm pushing.
first day all week of feeling a bit sluggish. not much of a run though, so i should be fresh tomorrow

up Broken Hills, to the La Jolla Half 9 mi mark, then Guy Fleming on the way down. 9.75 is the rough estimate, maybe a little more. Stoked on the way the week rounded out, it felt easy to do. Flying back tomorrow

First day of practice! 15 min wu, stretching etc., then out for the Elliot bridge loop. Faster than I've been running but I think that's more of a motivation issue. Everyone's looking good and seems stoked to be back.

Jen, Julia, and Condon all being there too makes the pack look promising. Half a mile to go before I hit my summer mileage goal of 550... I almost want to just go run a few laps right now, ha. Off to Maine tommorow!
recovery run before the drive to maine.

Gorgeous run, I really like the area. Funny little community race... the only time I've even seen people move backwards when told to line up by pace.
